summary:
The Sangam Period Tamil Kingdoms (c. 300–200 BC and continuing into the early centuries AD) represent the earliest documented civilization of southern India, characterized by sophisticated poetry, maritime trade, and the three Tamil kingdoms of the Cheras, Cholas, and Pandyas.

The Sangam period takes its name from legendary literary academies (sangams) where Tamil poets gathered under royal patronage. The corpus of Sangam literature — thousands of poems preserved in anthologies — provides a vivid picture of early Tamil society: its warrior culture, romantic ideals, elaborate social codes, and deep engagement with the sea. The three kingdoms competed for dominance of the southern tip of India while also trading extensively with Rome, Arabia, and Southeast Asia through their ports. Finds of Roman coins and amphora at Tamil Nadu sites confirm that this was the far end of a vast trade network connecting the Mediterranean to [[event:1110|Indian Ocean Trade Routes]]. Sangam-age ports like Muziri (Muchiri) were mentioned in Roman geographical sources as sources of spices, gems, and silks.
